system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 28: Rhythmizing Scale Sequences

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 28 — Rhythmizing Scale Sequences

_Pages 113-115._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Modal Transposition of Pattern

> First play the pattern starting from every degree of the scale (in other words modally transposing
> it). In the example below all of the notes are played over a dominant sus 4 chord. This allows you to
>
> hear the sound of the key without having to deal with any avoid notes.

**Significance (p. 113).** Modal sequence application across scale degrees over sus4.

## Omitting Notes

> Next leave out some of the notes within the pattern.

**Significance (p. 113).** Selective note omission as rhythmizing move.

## Groups of Seven via Omission

> Leaving out one note in two groups of four creates groups of seven.

**Significance (p. 113).** Specific omission generates odd-grouping polyrhythm.

## Rests Within Sequences

> Leave out some notes and add rests.

**Significance (p. 114).** Rests as active sequence-manipulation element.

## Expansive Sequence Hearing

> As you can see, this approach leads to a very expansive way of hearing sequences.

**Significance (p. 114).** Frames goal as aural expansion.

## Ascending/Descending Within Sequence

> Ascend or descend within the sequence.

**Significance (p. 114).** Directional variation rule.

## Skip Entry Points

> Skip up or down to any note in the scale to start the sequence.

**Significance (p. 114).** Free entry point on any scale degree.

## Polyrhythmic Application

> Try applying polyrhythmic approaches to the sequence.

**Significance (p. 114).** Polyrhythm layered onto scale sequences.

## Practice Methodology

> When practicing these exercises start
> otf with familiarizing yourself with the sequence and then work your way through the possibil-
> ities taking as many liberties as you'd like. Try composing 12 modal sequences and then playing
> each of them on one or more of the play along tracks.

**Significance (p. 115).** Internalize first, then expand freedom; 12-sequence target.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
